sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Back pain is common and is often due to benign musculoskeletal causes, but clinicians must recognise spinal emergencies and important non-spinal mimics that may progress to permanent neurological injury or life-threatening disease.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Recent onset pain without major trauma
- Severe or progressive pain
- Bilateral sciatic pain
- Urinary retention or incontinence
- Bowel incontinence
- Lower-extremity neurological deficit
- Fever, chills, night sweats, or weight loss
- Saddle sensory change

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- New, chronic, or previously diagnosed back pain can still represent a spinal emergency
- Red flags include cancer history, immunosuppression, infection risk, IV drug use, steroid use, and cauda equina features
- Consider visceral mimics such as pancreatitis, aortic aneurysm, renal colic, or cardiac causes
- Major trauma, significant neurological deficit, fever, or sepsis features should lower the threshold for transport and escalation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Complete vital signs and focused neurological assessment, including lower-limb power, sensation, and bladder or bowel red flags where relevant
- Position for comfort and avoid forced movement
- Provide simple or inhaled analgesia within scope
- Consider non-spinal causes if pain is severe, atypical, or associated with chest, abdominal, vascular, or systemic features
- Transport or escalate early if red flags, significant functional limitation, or uncontrolled pain are present

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access if analgesia, antiemetic therapy, or another serious diagnosis is being managed
- Consider titrated additional analgesia in line with local protocol and patient risk factors
- Consider antiemetic therapy if pain or opioid treatment is associated with nausea or vomiting
- Consider ECG or other condition-specific assessment if the presentation could represent vascular, cardiac, abdominal, or renal pathology
- Escalate rapidly if there is progressive neurology, sepsis concern, suspected cauda equina syndrome, or a major non-spinal mimic

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Assessment of back pain is complex and requires correlation of pain features, neurological findings, and red flags
- Full history, including change from baseline in chronic conditions, is important
